title: Huawei's Chip End Run and Iran's Cyber War
subhead: US semiconductor controls face their sharpest test yet as Huawei launches an AI chip competitive with restricted Nvidia hardware, while Iranian cyber operations expose critical infrastructure vulnerabilities across American cities.

sections.heading: Huawei's 950PR: Sanctions Breed a Rival
sections.body:
Huawei has launched the Ascend 950PR, a 1.56-petaflop AI inference chip delivering 2.8 times the FP4 performance of Nvidia's H20 — the most advanced chip legally available in China under US export controls. ByteDance has committed $5.6 billion in orders for 2026, with Huawei targeting 750,000 units this year. The chip exists because US sanctions forced Chinese companies to build alternatives — and now those alternatives are becoming competitive.

The 950PR is manufactured by SMIC at approximately 7nm process using DUVi multi-patterning on ASML NXT:1980Fi machines acquired before restrictions. It's roughly three generations behind Nvidia's current B200 in raw process technology, but for inference workloads — running AI models rather than training them — the gap narrows significantly. Huawei's CANN Next software stack targets 80% PyTorch compatibility, lowering the migration barrier from CUDA.

The power shift is structural: Nvidia's China revenue is projected to decline from $17.1 billion in FY2025 to $8-10 billion in FY2026, while Huawei's AI chip revenue could match or exceed it for the first time. American export controls designed to cap Chinese AI capability have instead accelerated domestic chip development to near-frontier competence.
sections.whyItMatters: China's largest AI companies are building infrastructure on domestic silicon, breaking the Nvidia dependency. The sanctions achieved exactly what they were designed to prevent: a self-sustaining Chinese AI chip ecosystem.

sections.heading: The DUVi Loophole: Making Chips Without EUV
sections.body:
An American Enterprise Institute report published this month documents a fundamental gap in US export controls: China doesn't need EUV lithography to make near-frontier chips — it has enough DUVi machines and willingness to accept lower yields. SMIC acquired approximately 90 ASML NXT:1980Fi tools in 2024 worth $5-7 billion, representing 20-25% of the global installed base of the most capable DUVi equipment. All were purchased legally before any DUVi restrictions.

SMIC's 7nm process (demonstrated in Huawei's Kirin 9000s) uses DUVi multi-patterning — exposing silicon multiple times to achieve features smaller than the light wavelength allows. The technique is slower and less yield-efficient than EUV, but it works. SMIC is now developing 5nm capability at approximately 20% yield, with Huawei targeting 1.6 million high-end logic dies in 2026 for AI accelerators.

A House bill introduced this month would close the gap by targeting capability-based thresholds rather than specific product names. The timeline determines whether SMIC completes its 5nm ramp before controls tighten.
sections.whyItMatters: The EUV export ban assumed DUVi couldn't make competitive chips. That assumption was wrong. China has a fleet of advanced DUVi machines and engineers who've mastered multi-patterning — a production path that current controls don't address.

sections.heading: Iranian Cyber Operations Target US Infrastructure
sections.body:
On April 7th — day 38 of Operation Epic Fury — CISA warned that Iranian-linked cyber actors had gained access to programmable logic controllers (PLCs) controlling American water and energy systems. Iran is conducting infrastructure reconnaissance on US municipal systems, learning how they work and demonstrating reach. The Aliquippa, Pennsylvania water system breach in 2023 showed the pattern: small municipalities lack cybersecurity expertise and funding, making them ideal targets for adversaries building knowledge of critical infrastructure vulnerabilities.

Seedworm (also known as MuddyWater) infiltrated networks of an American airport, a bank, and a defense contractor weeks before the conflict began. The Handala Hack Team, a front for Iran's Ministry of Intelligence and Security, launched a wiperware attack on Stryker, a Michigan medical technology company, postponing surgeries and disrupting device deliveries worldwide.

The conflict's cyber dimension continues regardless of any military ceasefire. Iran's strength is persistence and coercive signaling — creating disruption without exotic capabilities. As one Handala post stated: 'The cyber war did not begin with the military conflict, and it will not end with any military ceasefire.'
sections.whyItMatters: Iranian cyber actors are already inside US critical infrastructure networks. They're not launching Hollywood-style attacks because they don't need to — the reconnaissance itself generates fear and signals capability. Municipal water systems, hospitals, and local utilities are the soft underbelly of American infrastructure.

sections.heading: US Cyber Defenses Erode as Threats Rise
sections.body:
CISA issued its infrastructure warning just weeks after the Trump administration proposed cutting the agency's budget by $707 million and eliminating its election security program. FBI Director Kash Patel fired dozens of personnel from the counterintelligence unit monitoring Iranian threats days before the conflict began — the same unit investigating Trump's classified document case. The agencies responsible for defending critical infrastructure are being cut while adversaries are actively probing those systems.

Patel himself became a target: Handala leaked hundreds of his private emails and photos, with the group's website proclaiming the FBI director 'will now find his name among the list of successfully hacked victims.' The attack was confirmed by the FBI, though the hosting appeared to trace to Russia.

CISA lost approximately a third of its staff in Trump's first year, including the team responsible for testing the nation's security defenses. The current budget proposal would eliminate more than 700 additional positions.
sections.whyItMatters: A gap is opening between threat intensity and defensive capacity. Iranian actors are inside US networks; the agencies tasked with finding them are shrinking. The question isn't whether attacks will happen, but whether anyone is left to detect them.

sections.heading: DeepSeek V4: China's AI Models Keep Closing
sections.body:
DeepSeek released V4 on April 24th, offering a 1-million token context length at what the company calls 'drastically reduced compute and memory costs.' The model comes in Pro and Flash variants, with V4-Pro matching or approaching top-tier closed-source models like Gemini-3.1-Pro on world knowledge benchmarks. The cost-performance curve that made DeepSeek a global story in January 2025 continues: Chinese labs are delivering competitive models at lower inference cost than US rivals.

The release coincides with escalating accusations from the White House and US AI companies about 'model extraction attacks' — using thousands of queries to distill larger models into smaller ones. OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google have accused Chinese AI firms, including DeepSeek, of systematically extracting American model capabilities.

Italy, the United States, South Korea, and Germany have banned government agencies from using DeepSeek, citing national security and data transfer concerns. The model remains available on Hugging Face for download and adaptation.
sections.whyItMatters: China's AI labs aren't waiting for hardware parity. They're optimizing for the constraints they have: developing models that deliver competitive performance on domestic chips at lower cost. The open-source release model accelerates global adoption regardless of government bans.

sections.heading: Critical Minerals: US Leads, Europe Trails
sections.body:
The US is accelerating critical mineral development through the Defense Production Act and Inflation Reduction Act incentives, with domestic mining and processing capacity expanding for lithium, cobalt, and rare earths. Europe faces a structural disadvantage: environmental regulations and NIMBY politics have blocked mine development for decades, creating dependency on Chinese processing even for minerals extracted elsewhere.

China processes approximately 60-70% of the world's lithium and 85-90% of rare earths. The US is building alternative processing capacity in Australia, Canada, and domestically, but European projects face permitting timelines of 10-15 years. The result is a mineral supply chain where the US gains options while Europe remains dependent on Chinese-controlled processing infrastructure.

The EU's Critical Raw Materials Act aims for 10% domestic extraction by 2030, but current projects are insufficient to meet even modest targets without dramatic regulatory reform.
sections.whyItMatters: The energy transition runs on minerals Europe doesn't mine and can't process. While the US builds supply chain independence through legislation and investment, European industrial policy is paralyzed by permitting delays and local opposition. The green transition is creating new dependencies faster than Europe is breaking old ones.

bottomLine: US technology controls are achieving their stated goals — containment of Chinese AI capability — but producing the opposite structural outcome: a domestic Chinese chip industry now competitive for inference workloads and closing the gap on training. Huawei's 950PR, manufactured on machines legally acquired before restrictions, proves that controls drawn at specific technical thresholds create workarounds rather than walls. Meanwhile, Iranian cyber actors have demonstrated that America's critical infrastructure vulnerabilities are local and municipal — exactly where federal investment and expertise are weakest. The Trump administration's cuts to CISA and FBI counterintelligence units are reducing defensive capacity at the moment threat intensity is rising. The day's central pattern: technological chokepoints are leaky, and the leaks are producing the capabilities controls were designed to prevent.
sources: Tech-Insider (Huawei Ascend 950PR launch), American Enterprise Institute (DUVi lithography loophole), New Yorker (Iranian cyber operations), CISA advisory, Euronews (DeepSeek V4), Forbes (critical minerals), Time (rare earths), Ars Technica (Russia ASAT weapons)
